relatedChapters.title: Leydig Cell Hypoplasia (LCH): Two Published Cases of LHCGR Mutations in 46,XY DSD
Clinical Evaluation of Gonadal Dysgenesis: Five-Case Resident Training Module

items.title: How disorders of sex development are defined
items.content: Disorders of sex development (DSD) are congenital conditions in which chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex are incongruent, and the label also applies to some conditions without obvious genital ambiguity.
keyPoints: Mismatch can involve chromosomal, gonadal, or anatomical sex.
The spectrum includes cases without visible ambiguity.
Older labels like intersex are no longer preferred.
items.reference: DSD refers to congenital discordance among chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex and can include cases without overt ambiguity.

items.title: Why chromosomal DSD arises from nondisjunction or chimerism
items.content: Chromosomal DSD is caused by meiotic or mitotic nondisjunction leading to aneuploidy and mosaicism, or by chimerism when two genetically distinct cell lines coexist after double fertilization or early zygote fusion.
keyPoints: Meiotic nondisjunction yields classic sex chromosome aneuploidy.
Mitotic errors cause mosaic karyotypes.
Chimerism reflects two genetic origins in one individual.
items.reference: Chromosomal DSD results from meiotic or mitotic nondisjunction or from chimerism produced by double fertilization or early zygote fusion.

items.title: How mosaicism differs from chimerism in DSD
items.content: Mosaicism arises from a single zygote with post-zygotic mitotic errors, whereas chimerism reflects two distinct genetic lineages in one individual due to dispermy or early fusion of zygotes.
keyPoints: Mosaicism: one zygote, multiple cell lines.
Chimerism: two genetic origins.
Ovotesticular DSD is a classic example of chimerism.
items.reference: Mosaicism comes from one zygote with mitotic nondisjunction, while chimerism arises from two genetically distinct cell lines.

items.title: How sex determination differs from sex differentiation
items.content: Sex determination is the genetic decision of the bipotential gonad to become testis or ovary, while sex differentiation is the hormone-driven development of internal and external genitalia; both occur mainly during weeks 7 to 12 of gestation.
keyPoints: Determination sets gonadal fate.
Differentiation shapes internal and external anatomy.
The critical window is mid-first trimester.
items.reference: Sex determination establishes gonadal fate, whereas differentiation shapes internal and external genitalia during weeks 7 to 12.

items.title: How key genes guide testis and ovary development
items.content: Testis development is driven by genes such as SRY, SOX9, SF1, WT1, and DHH, while ovarian development requires active signaling from WNT4, FOXL2, and RSPO1 rather than passive default.
keyPoints: SRY initiates the testis pathway via SOX9.
Testis fate depends on multiple transcription factors.
Ovarian fate requires WNT4, FOXL2, and RSPO1.
items.reference: Testis determination depends on SRY, SOX9, SF1, WT1, and DHH, while ovarian development requires WNT4, FOXL2, and RSPO1.

items.title: How DAX1 dosage alters gonadal development
items.content: DAX1 on Xp20.3 is dose sensitive: one copy supports normal gonadal development, loss leads to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with adrenal hypoplasia, and duplication promotes gonadal dysgenesis.
keyPoints: DAX1 is critical for gonads and adrenal cortex.
Loss causes adrenal hypoplasia with hypogonadism.
Duplication can trigger gonadal dysgenesis.
items.reference: DAX1 is dose dependent; loss causes hypogonadism with adrenal hypoplasia, while duplication leads to gonadal dysgenesis.

items.title: How Wolffian and Mullerian ducts map to internal organs
items.content: Wolffian ducts form epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, and related structures in males, while Mullerian ducts form fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and upper vagina in females.
keyPoints: Wolffian ducts become male internal tract structures.
Mullerian ducts become female internal tract structures.
Both duct systems are present early in development.
items.reference: Wolffian derivatives include epididymis and vas deferens, whereas Mullerian derivatives include the uterus and fallopian tubes.

items.title: How external genitalia development depends on androgens
items.content: The genital tubercle, urogenital folds, and labioscrotal swellings differentiate into male structures with androgen exposure; in the absence of androgens they form the clitoris, labia minora, and labia majora.
keyPoints: Male differentiation requires androgen exposure.
Female differentiation occurs without androgen stimulation.
Common primordia underlie both pathways.
items.reference: External genital primordia form male structures with androgens and female structures when androgens are absent.

items.title: How AMH and testosterone shape male differentiation
items.content: Sertoli cell AMH induces Mullerian regression, Leydig cell testosterone stabilizes Wolffian ducts, and conversion to dihydrotestosterone drives external virilization because DHT has much higher androgen receptor affinity.
keyPoints: AMH causes Mullerian duct regression.
Testosterone preserves Wolffian ducts.
DHT mediates external virilization.
items.reference: AMH regresses Mullerian ducts, testosterone stabilizes Wolffian ducts, and DHT mediates external virilization.

items.title: How Prader staging, EMS, and EGS quantify atypical genitalia in infants
items.content:
Prader staging grades virilization in 46,XX infants with androgen excess. For quantitative documentation of external genital anatomy in DSD — especially 46,XY undervirilization — two parallel 0-to-12 scoring systems exist:

• External Masculinization Score (EMS) — Ahmed et al. (2000): grades degree of masculinization using male-oriented terminology (micropenis, hypospadias). Score 12 = typical fully masculinized male external genitalia; 0 = typical female phenotype.

• External Genitalia Score (EGS) — van der Zwan et al. (2015): maps to the identical mathematical structure of EMS (0–12) but uses strictly descriptive, gender-neutral embryological anatomy (genital tubercle, labioscrotal fusion, urethral meatus on the tubercle). Score 0 = typical female genitalia; 12 = typical male genitalia.

Both tools document baseline anatomy, track change after hormonal stimulation (e.g. testosterone or DHT), and communicate findings quantitatively between endocrinologists, geneticists, and surgeons. EGS is preferred before definitive diagnosis or sex of rearing is assigned because EMS terminology embeds a male baseline.
keyPoints: Prader = 46,XX virilization grading.
EMS (Ahmed 2000) and EGS (van der Zwan 2015) both range 0–12.
EGS = same math, gender-neutral nomenclature.
items.reference: EMS grades undervirilization in 46,XY DSD with male score 12 and female score 0; EGS maps to the same 0–12 structure using gender-neutral embryological anatomy.

items.title: How to calculate the External Masculinization Score (EMS)
items.content:
EMS (Ahmed et al., 2000) quantifies masculinization in 46,XY ambiguous genitalia. Maximum score 12. Component scores:

Labioscrotal fusion: complete normal male scrotum 3; partial fusion 1.5; no fusion (female phenotype) 0.

Phallus size: absent/normal size 3; micropenis present 0.

Urethral meatus: tip of phallus 3; glandular/coronal hypospadias 2; penile hypospadias 1; perineal/female configuration 0.

Right gonad: palpable in scrotum 1.5; inguinal/abdominal non-palpable 0.

Left gonad: palpable in scrotum 1.5; inguinal/abdominal non-palpable 0.

Clinical utility: baseline documentation, tracking response to DHT or testosterone stimulation, and standardized communication between specialists. Limitation: terms like masculinization, micropenis, and hypospadias assume a male reference frame.
keyPoints: Five domains — max 12 points.
Fusion 3 + phallus 3 + meatus 3 + gonads 3.
Useful for tracking post-stimulation change.
items.reference: EMS ranges from 0 to 12, where 12 represents typical, fully masculinized male external genitalia, and lower scores indicate varying degrees of undervirilization.

items.title: How to calculate the External Genitalia Score (EGS)
items.content:
EGS (van der Zwan et al., 2015) uses the same mathematical structure as EMS (0–12) with gender-neutral embryological descriptors:

Labioscrotal fusion: complete fusion 3; partial fusion 1.5; no fusion 0.

Genital tubercle size: normal length 3; reduced length 0.

Urethral meatus location: tip of genital tubercle 3; proximal on tubercle 2; base of tubercle 1; perineal (typical female) 0.

Right gonad: palpable in labioscrotal fold 1.5; non-palpable 0.

Left gonad: palpable in labioscrotal fold 1.5; non-palpable 0.

Score 0 = typical female external genitalia; 12 = typical male. Applicable to any infant with DSD — 46,XX, 46,XY, or mosaic — without prematurely imposing male or female clinical paradigms. Serves the same diagnostic and longitudinal tracking purpose as EMS with standardized nomenclature.
keyPoints: Identical point structure to EMS.
Genital tubercle — not micropenis.
Gender-neutral — any karyotype before sex of rearing.
items.reference: The EGS maps exactly to the mathematical structure of the EMS (ranging from 0 to 12) but utilizes strictly descriptive, gender-neutral embryological anatomy.

items.title: Why EGS was developed to address gender bias in EMS terminology
items.content:
EMS terminology — masculinization, micropenis, hypospadias — assumes a male anatomical baseline. Before definitive diagnosis, karyotype confirmation, or sex-of-rearing decisions, this language can prematurely frame the infant within a male clinical paradigm and create psychological and communication problems for families and multidisciplinary teams.

EGS replaces gendered labels with embryological descriptors: genital tubercle (not penis/clitoris dichotomy in scoring), labioscrotal fold (not scrotum/labia), and urethral meatus position on the tubercle (not hypospadias grading). Because the numeric weights are identical, EMS and EGS scores are directly comparable for the same examination — allowing transition to EMS in research contexts while using EGS in early neutral documentation.

Practical rule: use EGS (or neutral prose) at initial presentation of atypical genitalia; EMS remains valid for 46,XY undervirilization tracking once the diagnostic framework is established.
keyPoints: EMS embeds male-reference terminology.
EGS = neutral embryology, same 0–12 math.
EGS preferred before sex of rearing assigned.
items.reference: Using terms like masculinization, micropenis, or hypospadias assumes a male baseline, which is problematic before a definitive diagnosis or sex of rearing is assigned.

items.title: When to investigate suspected DSD — EMS, EGS, and clinical triggers
items.content:
DSD clinical evaluation criteria (consensus guideline) define who needs investigation vs who can avoid unnecessary detailed workup:

Score thresholds: EMS <11 or EGS <10.5 → consider investigation for suspected DSD.

Boys — warrant investigation: isolated perineal hypospadias; any familial hypospadias; bilateral undescended testes; micropenis (stretched penile length <2.5 SD, with contemporary studies using <2 cm as a reasonable newborn cut-off, e.g. 1.8 cm is actionable).

Boys — generally avoid unnecessary detailed investigation if isolated benign findings only: glandular hypospadias, coronal hypospadias, mid-shaft hypospadias (without other features), unilateral inguinal testis, retractile or acquired undescended testes.

Girls — warrant investigation: isolated clitoromegaly; clitoral length >8 mm (length does not seem dependent on gestational age).

Lower threshold for investigation: family history of any familial hypospadias, consanguinity, stillbirths, multiple miscarriages, need for steroid replacement, unexplained deaths; associated systemic metabolic disorder; dysmorphic features (XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition in ~25% of affected cases).

Purpose of explicit cut-offs: isolate infants who need evaluation while avoiding unnecessary investigation of boys with isolated benign findings.
keyPoints: EMS <11 or EGS <10.5 → investigate.
Perineal hypospadias, bilateral cryptorchidism, micropenis, clitoromegaly → investigate.
Isolated glandular/mid-shaft hypospadias → avoid unnecessary workup.
items.reference: An EMS below 11 and an EGS below 10.5 require consideration for investigation for suspected DSD.

items.title: Why explicit DSD investigation thresholds prevent over-investigation
items.content:
Without defined cut-offs, every minor genital variant triggers karyotype, hormone panels, and imaging — burdening families and services. The guideline separates high-yield triggers (EMS <11, EGS <10.5, perineal hypospadias, bilateral undescended testes, micropenis <2 cm, clitoral length >8 mm, isolated clitoromegaly) from low-yield isolated findings (glandular or mid-shaft hypospadias alone, unilateral inguinal testis).

Family and systemic red flags (consanguinity, stillbirths, miscarriages, steroid replacement in family, metabolic disorder, dysmorphism) lower the threshold even when anatomy is mild — reflecting that ~25% of XY DSD occurs within complex multi-system conditions. Clinical reasoning: match investigation intensity to pre-test probability.
keyPoints: Cut-offs reduce unnecessary testing of benign isolates.
Family/systemic flags lower threshold.
~25% XY DSD is multi-system — dysmorphism matters.
items.reference: To isolate patients requiring evaluation, thereby avoiding unnecessary detailed investigations of boys with isolated benign findings like unilateral inguinal testis.

items.title: How birthweight, dysmorphism, and family history modify the XY DSD workup
items.content:
XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition in approximately 25% of affected cases — so dysmorphic features on thorough examination lower the threshold for investigation even when genital anomalies are mild.

Birthweight is clinically important: knowledge of birthweight in XY DSD is very helpful given the well-reported association between low birthweight, intra-uterine growth retardation (IUGR), and XY DSD. An infant who is small for gestational age with documented maternal severe IUGR should prompt fuller evaluation alongside genital findings.

Family history factors that lower the threshold for investigation (work up milder genital findings more aggressively): consanguinity; family history of genital surgery, hernias, fertility problems, or delayed puberty; stillbirths; multiple miscarriages; unexplained deaths in infancy; need for steroid replacement in a relative. Coexisting systemic metabolic disorder similarly lowers threshold — investigate borderline genital anomalies that might otherwise be observed.

Practical meaning of "lower the threshold": initiate comprehensive DSD workup with milder or borderline genital findings when these modifiers are present; do not wait for severe or unambiguous anatomy alone.
keyPoints: ~25% XY DSD is multi-system — examine for dysmorphism.
Low birthweight/IUGR associates with XY DSD — record birthweight.
Consanguinity, stillbirths, steroid replacement in family → lower threshold.
items.reference: Knowledge of birth-weight in XY DSD is very helpful given the well-reported association between low birthweight, intra-uterine growth retardation and XY DSD.

items.title: How to structure first-tier investigations for atypical genitalia in the newborn
items.content:
First-tier investigations for an infant with atypical genitalia should define sex chromosomes (PCR or FISH using Y and X-specific markers), delineate internal genitalia by pelvic ultrasound, and rapidly screen for the commonest life-threatening cause — 21-hydroxylase deficiency CAH.

Biochemical first tier: plasma glucose, serum 17-OHP, and serial measurement of electrolytes. Prioritize exclusion of 21-OHD because it is the commonest cause of a life-threatening condition associated with atypical genitalia in the newborn.

Timing pitfalls: serum 17-OHP is usually unreliable before 36 hours of age — a 12-hour draw can mislead. In salt-losing CAH, serum electrolytes usually do not become abnormal before day 4 of life — normal electrolytes at 48 hours provide false reassurance. 17-OHP may be falsely elevated in premature or sick neonates — interpret with caution, not knee-jerk diagnosis.

In specialist centres, PCR/FISH and serum 17-OHP results should be available within a maximum of two working days. Baseline or stimulated serum cortisol can be difficult to interpret in the newborn, especially in the premature infant.
keyPoints: First tier: glucose, 17-OHP, serial electrolytes + karyotype + pelvic US.
17-OHP unreliable <36 h; electrolytes often normal until day 4+.
21-OHD = commonest life-threatening cause — prioritize.
items.reference: First tier should include plasma glucose, serum 17OHP and serial measurement of electrolytes; serum 17OHP is usually unreliable before the age of 36 hours.

items.title: Why serum and urine must be stored before emergency steroid therapy in suspected CAH
items.content:
When immediate life-saving steroid hormone replacement is required for suspected CAH, further serum and urine samples of sufficient volume must be collected and stored before starting therapy — otherwise post-treatment samples cannot support accurate diagnosis.

If blood volume is limited before emergency steroids, allocate assays in strict priority order: (1) 17-OHP, (2) testosterone, (3) androstenedione, (4) renin activity, (5) aldosterone. Do not delay hydrocortisone for a complete panel when the infant is in extremis — but never start steroids without saving diagnostic material when clinically feasible.

Pelvic ultrasound delineates internal genitalia in bilateral impalpable gonads — it does not replace biochemical CAH screening or karyotype.
keyPoints: Store serum + urine before first steroid dose.
Limited volume priority: 17-OHP → testosterone → androstenedione → renin → aldosterone.
US = internal genitalia; not a substitute for 17-OHP/electrolytes.
items.reference: Further serum and urine samples should be collected and stored before starting therapy; order of priority: 17OHP, testosterone, androstenedione, renin activity, and aldosterone.

items.title: How timing of prenatal androgens drives virilization
items.content: Androgen exposure before 12 weeks of gestation leads to clitoromegaly, posterior labial fusion, and a single urogenital opening, whereas exposure after 12 weeks generally causes isolated clitoromegaly.
keyPoints: Early exposure causes fusion and single opening.
Later exposure tends to cause isolated clitoromegaly.
Clitoral length of 9 mm or more is concerning.
items.reference: Androgen exposure before 12 weeks causes clitoromegaly with posterior fusion and a single opening, while later exposure causes isolated clitoromegaly.

items.title: Why a blind vaginal pouch develops in some 46,XY DSD
items.content: A blind vaginal pouch forms when AMH remains intact but testosterone action is impaired, leading to Mullerian regression with partial urogenital sinus differentiation; causes include CAIS, 17alpha-hydroxylase deficiency, 17beta-HSD3 deficiency, and 5alpha-reductase deficiency.
keyPoints: AMH regression preserves only lower vaginal segment.
Defective androgen action prevents full male differentiation.
Several androgen biosynthesis or action defects can cause it.
items.reference: Blind vaginal pouch occurs when AMH is preserved but testosterone action is impaired, as in CAIS or androgen biosynthetic defects.

items.title: Why both Wolffian and Mullerian derivatives can persist
items.content: Persistent Mullerian duct syndrome, ovotesticular DSD, and mixed gonadal dysgenesis can yield both duct systems because AMH action is insufficient or gonadal tissue is asymmetric.
keyPoints: PMDS reflects impaired AMH secretion or action.
OT-DSD and MGD often show asymmetric gonads.
Duct derivatives follow the side-specific gonad.
items.reference: Both duct systems can be present in PMDS, OT-DSD, or MGD when AMH action is inadequate or gonads are asymmetric.

items.title: How etiologies of 46,XY DSD are organized
items.content: 46,XY DSD stems from impaired testis development, androgen biosynthesis, or androgen action, and female external genitalia can be seen with complete gonadal dysgenesis, CAIS, or severe steroidogenic defects.
keyPoints: Major groups: testis development, biosynthesis, action.
Female external genitalia suggests severe defects.
Ambiguity can occur across all categories.
items.reference: 46,XY DSD includes testis development disorders, androgen biosynthetic defects, and androgen resistance; female genitalia can occur in severe forms.

items.title: How etiologies of 46,XX DSD are organized
items.content: 46,XX DSD arises from disorders of gonadal development or androgen excess; strong androgen exposure during weeks 8 to 12 can produce an apparently male phenotype even without testes.
keyPoints: Gonadal development disorders include SRY translocation.
Androgen excess causes virilization.
Early exposure yields the most masculinized phenotype.
items.reference: 46,XX DSD results from gonadal development defects or androgen excess, and early androgen exposure can produce a male-appearing phenotype.

items.title: How micropenis is defined and what it implies
items.content: Micropenis is defined statistically as stretched penile length below minus 2.5 standard deviations for age. Contemporary studies use less than 2 cm as a reasonable absolute cut-off in the newborn — e.g. 1.8 cm is clinically actionable and warrants investigation for suspected DSD per evaluation criteria. Older teaching often cites ~2.5 cm at term as approximate -2.5 SD. Micropenis suggests impaired androgen synthesis/action or pituitary disease and requires investigation even when hypospadias is absent.
keyPoints: Statistical definition: <2.5 SD stretched penile length.
Newborn practical cut-off: <2 cm (e.g. 1.8 cm actionable).
Investigate for DSD — not a benign isolated finding.
items.reference: Micropenis is a stretched penile length of less than 2.5 SD from the mean; less than 2cm is a reasonable cut-off in a newborn.

items.title: How hypospadias is classified and linked to DSD
items.content: Hypospadias is an ectopic urethral meatus on the ventral penis, classified as distal, mid, or proximal; proximal forms are more often associated with DSD and may coexist with chordee.
keyPoints: Distal is most common.
Proximal hypospadias raises DSD concern.
Chordee and dorsal hood are common associations.
items.reference: Hypospadias is a ventral meatus classified as distal, mid, or proximal, and proximal forms are more often associated with DSD.

items.title: How chordee develops in undervirilized 46,XY DSD
items.content: Chordee results from ventral penile tissues lagging behind dorsal corporal growth, likely due to lower androgen effect on urethral and spongiosal development.
keyPoints: Ventral tissues are underdeveloped.
Dorsal corporal growth predominates.
Androgen action is critical for urethral development.
items.reference: Chordee reflects underdevelopment of ventral tissues relative to dorsal corpora in undervirilized 46,XY DSD.

items.title: How cryptorchidism is defined and distributed
items.content: Cryptorchidism is failure of one or both testes to descend into the scrotum, usually resolving by 3 months of age, and most undescended testes are located high scrotal or inguinal.
keyPoints: Most spontaneous descent occurs by 3 months.
Persistent undescended testes are uncommon after 6 to 9 months.
High scrotal and inguinal locations are most common.
items.reference: Cryptorchidism is undescended testes, often descending by 3 months, and commonly located high scrotal or inguinal.

items.title: Why cryptorchidism with hypospadias raises DSD and chromosomal anomaly risk
items.content:
Isolated cryptorchidism is usually idiopathic, but in boys with atypical genitalia the probability of an underlying chromosomal anomaly rises with anatomical complexity: approximately 3% with isolated cryptorchidism, 7% with isolated hypospadias, and 13% when cryptorchidism and hypospadias coexist. The combination significantly increases DSD likelihood and suggests etiologies such as PAIS, androgen biosynthetic defects, mixed gonadal dysgenesis, or ovotesticular DSD.

Once 46,XY karyotype is confirmed, comprehensive endocrine and genetic investigations still yield a definitive abnormality in at least a quarter of cases — underscoring that normal XY chromosomes do not exclude DSD and that thorough biochemical and genetic workup remains mandatory.
keyPoints: Chromosomal anomaly risk: isolated cryptorchidism ~3%, hypospadias ~7%, both ~13%.
Cryptorchidism + hypospadias = highest chromosomal risk among these patterns.
46,XY confirmed: endocrine/genetic diagnosis in ≥25% with full workup.
items.reference: In boys with atypical genitalia, a chromosomal anomaly may be present in approximately 3% of those with isolated cryptorchidism, 7% of those with hypospadias and 13% of those with a combination of cryptorchidism and hypospadias.

items.title: How to distinguish anorchia from bilateral cryptorchidism
items.content: High gonadotropins with low testosterone suggest absent or dysgenetic testes, while low gonadotropins favor cryptorchidism due to central hypogonadism; AMH or inhibin B absence supports anorchia.
keyPoints: High LH/FSH with low testosterone suggests anorchia.
AMH and inhibin B help confirm testis absence.
hCG testing is useful in prepubertal children.
items.reference: Anorchia is suggested by high gonadotropins with low testosterone and undetectable AMH or inhibin B.

items.title: How hCG stimulation testing is used in DSD
items.content: Short-course hCG testing measures androgen response to distinguish androgen biosynthetic defects, 5alpha-reductase deficiency, and androgen resistance; T/DHT ratio above 10 suggests 5alpha-reductase deficiency, while T/A ratio below 0.8 with high androstenedione supports 17beta-HSD3 deficiency.
keyPoints: hCG is given for 3 days with pre and post testing.
T/DHT ratio greater than 10 favors 5alpha-reductase deficiency.
Low T/A ratio with high androstenedione favors 17beta-HSD3 deficiency.
items.reference: hCG testing differentiates androgen biosynthesis and action defects, using ratios such as T/DHT >10 or T/A <0.8 with high androstenedione.

items.title: How the newborn DSD evaluation starts with gonad palpation
items.content: In neonatal genital ambiguity, palpation of gonads guides the workup: palpable gonads suggest testes or ovotestes, nonpalpable gonads suggest undervirilized 46,XY or virilized 46,XX, and asymmetric findings suggest MGD or OT-DSD.
keyPoints: Palpable gonads imply testicular tissue.
Nonpalpable gonads broaden the differential.
Asymmetry raises suspicion for MGD or OT-DSD.
items.reference: Palpable gonads point to testes or ovotestes, nonpalpable gonads suggest 46,XY undervirilization or 46,XX virilization, and asymmetry suggests MGD or OT-DSD.

items.title: How psychosexual development is assessed
items.content: Psychosexual development comprises gender identity (self-perceived gender), gender role (behavioral expression), and gender orientation (sexual partner preference).
keyPoints: Identity is the internal sense of gender.
Role reflects social behavior and expression.
Orientation refers to partner preference.
items.reference: Psychosexual development includes gender identity, gender role, and gender orientation.

items.title: How gender dysphoria is defined in DSD care
items.content: Gender dysphoria describes significant distress from mismatch between gender identity and assigned sex of rearing, and it may occur with or without DSD.
keyPoints: Distress is the defining feature.
Mismatch involves identity and assigned sex.
It is not limited to DSD diagnoses.
items.reference: Gender dysphoria is distress caused by discordance between gender identity and assigned sex, and can occur with or without DSD.

items.title: How timing of hypospadias repair is determined
items.content: Hypospadias repair is ideally performed between 6 and 12 months of age to support psychosocial development and tissue pliability, and proximal lesions with chordee often require two-stage reconstruction.
keyPoints: Preferred age window is 6 to 12 months.
Early repair aligns with gender identity formation.
Proximal hypospadias may need staged repair.
items.reference: Hypospadias repair is ideally done at 6 to 12 months, with proximal lesions often needing two-stage reconstruction.

items.title: How gonadectomy decisions balance cancer risk and sex of rearing
items.content: Gonadectomy is recommended when tumor risk is high or when gonads conflict with sex of rearing, such as in 46,XY gonadal dysgenesis or MGD, while CAIS and OT-DSD require individualized timing and surveillance.
keyPoints: High-risk gonads merit early removal.
Sex of rearing influences the decision.
Lower-risk conditions may allow delayed surgery.
items.reference: Gonadectomy is advised for high-risk gonads or discordant sex of rearing, while lower-risk conditions may allow delayed removal with surveillance.

items.title: How XX male syndrome presents clinically
items.content: 46,XX male syndrome results from SRY translocation to the X chromosome, leading to a male phenotype with small testes, infertility, gynecomastia, and elevated FSH from germ cell failure.
keyPoints: SRY translocation drives male phenotype.
Testes are small with primary infertility.
FSH is consistently elevated.
items.reference: XX male syndrome is caused by SRY translocation and presents with male phenotype, small testes, infertility, gynecomastia, and elevated FSH.

items.question: A 20-year-old reared as female has primary amenorrhea, genital ambiguity, bilateral palpable gonads, 46,XY karyotype, high androstenedione with low testosterone, and a low T/A ratio after hCG. The most likely diagnosis is:
items.explanation: High androstenedione with low testosterone and low T/A ratio is typical of 17beta-HSD3 deficiency.
items.reference: Low testosterone with high androstenedione and low T/A ratio after hCG supports 17beta-HSD3 deficiency.

items.question: Disorders of sex development are defined as congenital conditions with discordance between:
options: Gonadal size and body mass index
items.explanation: DSD refers to mismatch among chromosomal, gonadal, and phenotypic sex.
items.reference: DSD are congenital disorders with discordance between chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex.

items.question: Chromosomal DSD most commonly results from:
options: Meiotic or mitotic nondisjunction and chimerism
Somatic point mutations in the androgen receptor
items.explanation: Nondisjunction and chimerism are key mechanisms.
items.reference: Chromosomal DSD results from nondisjunction during meiosis or mitosis or from chimerism.

items.question: Which statement best distinguishes mosaicism from chimerism?
options: Mosaicism comes from one zygote; chimerism from two genetic origins
Mosaicism requires two zygotes; chimerism requires one
Mosaicism is always lethal; chimerism is always benign
items.explanation: Mosaicism is one zygote with multiple cell lines, while chimerism involves two genetic origins.
items.reference: Mosaicism originates from one zygote, whereas chimerism reflects two genetically distinct cell lines.

items.question: Sex determination refers to:
options: Gonadal differentiation into testis or ovary
items.explanation: Determination is the decision of gonadal fate.
items.reference: Sex determination is the development of a bipotential gonad into testis or ovary.

items.question: Duplication of DAX1 most strongly predisposes to:
items.explanation: DAX1 is dose sensitive and duplication can impair gonadal development.
items.reference: Duplication of DAX1 is associated with gonadal dysgenesis.

items.question: Which structure is a Wolffian duct derivative in males?
items.explanation: Epididymis is derived from the Wolffian duct.
items.reference: Wolffian duct derivatives include the epididymis and vas deferens.

items.question: Anti-Mullerian hormone produced by Sertoli cells causes:
items.explanation: AMH drives Mullerian duct regression.
items.reference: AMH from Sertoli cells regresses Mullerian ducts.

items.question: External genital virilization in the embryo relies primarily on:
items.explanation: DHT has high androgen receptor affinity and drives virilization.
items.reference: External genital differentiation requires testosterone conversion to DHT.

items.question: The external masculinization score (EMS) for a typical male newborn is:
items.explanation: EMS (Ahmed et al., 2000) ranges from 0 (typical female phenotype) to 12 (fully masculinized male external genitalia).
items.reference: EMS score is 12 for a normal male external genitalia and 0 for a normal female.

items.question: Androgen exposure before 12 weeks of gestation in a 46,XX fetus most likely results in:
options: Clitoromegaly with posterior labial fusion and a single urogenital opening
items.explanation: Early exposure produces more extensive virilization.
items.reference: Androgen exposure before 12 weeks causes clitoromegaly, posterior fusion, and a single urogenital opening.

items.question: A blind vaginal pouch in a 46,XY individual is most consistent with:
options: Defective androgen action with preserved AMH
Early rupture of the genital sinus
items.explanation: AMH preserves Mullerian regression while impaired androgen action prevents full male differentiation.
items.reference: Blind vaginal pouch reflects preserved AMH with impaired testosterone action.

items.question: Presence of both Wolffian and Mullerian derivatives suggests:
items.explanation: PMDS is characterized by retained Mullerian structures in a male.
items.reference: Both duct systems can be present in PMDS, OT-DSD, or MGD.

items.question: Female external genitalia in a 46,XY individual is most commonly seen with:
options: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21alpha-hydroxylase deficiency
items.explanation: CAIS causes female external genitalia with absent Mullerian structures.
items.reference: 46,XY individuals with CAIS typically have female external genitalia.

items.question: The most common cause of ambiguous genitalia in a 46,XX newborn is:
options: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21alpha-hydroxylase deficiency
items.explanation: 21alpha-hydroxylase deficiency is the most common cause.
items.reference: CAH due to 21alpha-hydroxylase deficiency is the most common cause of virilization in 46,XX infants.

items.question: A 46,XX individual with apparently male external genitalia is most likely to have:
options: SRY gene translocation to the X chromosome
items.explanation: SRY translocation can produce a male phenotype in 46,XX.
items.reference: SRY translocation to the X chromosome can cause 46,XX testicular DSD.

items.question: Micropenis in a term newborn is typically defined as stretched penile length:
items.explanation: The cutoff is about 2.5 cm in term newborns.
items.reference: Micropenis is stretched penile length below -2.5 SD, about 2.5 cm in term newborns.

items.question: The most common type of hypospadias is:
items.explanation: Distal hypospadias accounts for the majority of cases.
items.reference: Distal hypospadias is the most common form.

items.question: Chordee in undervirilized 46,XY DSD occurs because:
options: Ventral penile tissues are underdeveloped compared with dorsal corpora
Penile skin is fused to the scrotum only
items.explanation: Disproportionate growth of dorsal vs ventral tissues produces curvature.
items.reference: Chordee reflects underdevelopment of ventral tissues relative to dorsal corpora.

items.question: Most spontaneous testicular descent in cryptorchidism occurs by:
items.explanation: Most descent occurs within the first 3 months.
items.reference: Spontaneous descent of testes usually occurs by 1 to 3 months of age.

items.question: After hCG stimulation, a testosterone to dihydrotestosterone ratio greater than 10 suggests:
items.explanation: T/DHT >10 is characteristic of 5alpha-reductase deficiency.
items.reference: A T/DHT ratio greater than 10 after hCG indicates 5alpha-reductase type 2 deficiency.

items.question: High androstenedione with a T/A ratio below 0.8 after hCG most strongly suggests:
items.explanation: Low T/A ratio with high androstenedione supports 17beta-HSD3 deficiency.
items.reference: T/A ratio below 0.8 with elevated androstenedione indicates 17beta-HSD3 deficiency.

items.question: Which CAH variant can produce genital ambiguity in both 46,XX and 46,XY individuals?
items.explanation: POR deficiency is the variant associated with ambiguity in both sexes.
items.reference: P450 oxidoreductase deficiency is the CAH variant associated with ambiguity in both sexes.

items.question: Placental aromatase deficiency is classically associated with:
items.explanation: Excess fetal androgens virilize both mother and female fetus.
items.reference: Placental aromatase deficiency leads to maternal virilization and virilization of 46,XX fetuses.

items.question: The typical genetic mechanism in XX male syndrome is:
options: SRY translocation to the X chromosome
Duplication of the androgen receptor gene
items.explanation: SRY translocation produces a male phenotype in 46,XX.
items.reference: XX male syndrome is caused by SRY translocation to the X chromosome.

items.question: Corrective surgery for hypospadias is ideally performed at:
items.explanation: Repair in infancy aligns with tissue pliability and psychosocial considerations.
items.reference: Hypospadias repair is ideally performed between 6 and 12 months.

items.question: In the EMS, complete labioscrotal fusion (normal male scrotum) contributes how many points?
items.explanation: Labioscrotal fusion: complete 3, partial 1.5, none 0 — one of five domains summing to max 12.
items.reference: Labioscrotal Fusion: Complete (normal male scrotum) 3; Partial fusion 1.5; No fusion (female phenotype) 0.

items.question: The External Genitalia Score (EGS) differs from EMS primarily because it:
options: Uses gender-neutral embryological descriptors with the same 0–12 point structure as EMS
Replaces gonadal palpation with ultrasound scoring
items.explanation: EGS (van der Zwan 2015) maps identically to EMS mathematically but uses neutral terms (genital tubercle, labioscrotal fold) — applicable before sex of rearing is assigned.
items.reference: The EGS maps exactly to the mathematical structure of the EMS (ranging from 0 to 12) but utilizes strictly descriptive, gender-neutral embryological anatomy.

items.question: A 46,XY infant with perineal urethral opening, no labioscrotal fusion, micropenis, and bilateral non-palpable gonads would score 0 on which EMS/EGS domains?
options: Labioscrotal fusion, phallus/tubercle size, and urethral meatus only
Labioscrotal fusion, phallus/tubercle size, urethral meatus, and both gonads
items.explanation: No fusion 0 + micropenis/reduced tubercle 0 + perineal meatus 0 + non-palpable gonads 0 each = 0 on all five domains (total EMS/EGS 0).
items.reference: No fusion 0; micropenis/reduced genital tubercle 0; perineal meatus 0; non-palpable gonads 0.

items.question: A newborn has glandular/coronal hypospadias on EMS assessment. The urethral meatus domain score is:
items.explanation: EMS urethral meatus: tip 3, glandular/coronal 2, penile 1, perineal/female 0.
items.reference: Urethral Meatus Location: Glandular/Coronal hypospadias 2.

items.question: On EMS, penile hypospadias (meatus on penile shaft) scores:
items.explanation: Penile hypospadias = 1 point; glandular/coronal = 2; tip = 3; perineal = 0.
items.reference: Urethral Meatus Location: Penile hypospadias 1.

items.question: Partial labioscrotal fusion scores how many points on both EMS and EGS?
items.explanation: Partial fusion = 1.5 on both systems; complete = 3; none = 0.
items.reference: Labioscrotal Fusion: Partial fusion 1.5.

items.question: A 46,XY infant has complete labioscrotal fusion, normal phallus size, urethral meatus at the tip, and one palpable gonad in the scrotum with a non-palpable contralateral gonad. The EMS total is:
items.explanation: Fusion 3 + phallus 3 + meatus 3 + one scrotal gonad 1.5 + one non-palpable 0 = 10.5.
items.reference: EMS component scores: fusion 3, phallus 3, meatus 3, palpable scrotal gonad 1.5 each.

items.question: A 46,XY infant has partial labioscrotal fusion, micropenis, penile hypospadias, and bilateral non-palpable gonads. The EMS total score is:
items.explanation: Partial fusion 1.5 + micropenis 0 + penile meatus 1 + gonads 0 + 0 = 2.5.
items.reference: Partial fusion 1.5; micropenis 0; penile hypospadias 1; non-palpable gonads 0.

items.question: A 46,XY infant has complete scrotal fusion, normal phallus, coronal hypospadias, and both gonads palpable in the scrotum. The EMS total is:
items.explanation: Fusion 3 + phallus 3 + glandular/coronal meatus 2 + both scrotal gonads 3 = 11.
items.reference: Complete fusion 3; normal phallus 3; glandular/coronal meatus 2; scrotal gonads 1.5 each.

items.question: On EGS, a urethral opening at the base of the genital tubercle scores:
items.explanation: EGS meatus: tip 3, proximal on tubercle 2, base of tubercle 1, perineal 0 — parallels EMS penile/base category.
items.reference: Urethral Meatus Location: Base of the genital tubercle 1.

items.question: The EGS replaces the EMS phallus-size domain with assessment of:
items.explanation: EGS uses genital tubercle length — normal 3, reduced 0 — avoiding the term micropenis.
items.reference: Genital Tubercle Size: Normal length 3; Reduced length 0.

items.question: A newborn with atypical genitalia is first seen before karyotype results are available. The most appropriate quantitative scoring system is:
options: EMS, because it is the older validated standard
EGS, because it uses gender-neutral embryological descriptors
No scoring until sex of rearing is assigned
items.explanation: EGS (van der Zwan 2015) avoids male-baseline terminology before diagnosis and sex-of-rearing decisions; same 0–12 math as EMS.
items.reference: EGS allows scoring any infant with DSD without prematurely imposing male or female clinical paradigms.

items.question: The primary clinical utility of serial EMS or EGS measurement after short-course testosterone or DHT stimulation is to:
options: Document quantitatively whether external genital anatomy responds to androgen
items.explanation: Both scores track anatomical change post-stimulation — baseline vs follow-up comparison for androgen responsiveness.
items.reference: EMS is useful for documenting baseline anatomy and tracking changes post-hormonal stimulation.

items.question: Prader staging is preferred over EMS/EGS for quantifying external genitalia in which population?
options: 46,XX infants with virilization from androgen excess
All DSD infants regardless of karyotype
items.explanation: Prader grades virilization in 46,XX DSD; EMS/EGS quantify undervirilization or neutral anatomy scoring in atypical genitalia workup.
items.reference: Prader staging grades virilization in 46,XX infants.

items.question: A typical female external genital phenotype scores how many points on EMS or EGS?
items.explanation: Score 0 = no fusion, reduced/absent tubercle relative to male norm, perineal meatus, non-palpable gonads — typical female configuration.
items.reference: A score of 0 represents typical female genitalia.

items.question: What is the approximate probability of identifying a chromosomal anomaly in a boy presenting solely with isolated cryptorchidism?
items.explanation: Isolated cryptorchidism in boys with atypical genitalia carries approximately a 3% risk of underlying chromosomal anomaly — lower than isolated hypospadias (7%) or the combination (13%).
items.reference: A chromosomal anomaly may be present in approximately 3% of those with isolated cryptorchidism.

items.question: Among boys with atypical genitalia, which physical finding carries the highest approximate risk (~13%) for an underlying chromosomal anomaly?
options: A combination of cryptorchidism and hypospadias
items.explanation: Cryptorchidism plus hypospadias together carry ~13% chromosomal anomaly risk vs 3% (isolated cryptorchidism) and 7% (isolated hypospadias).
items.reference: 13% of those with a combination of cryptorchidism and hypospadias.

items.question: In a boy with atypical genitalia and confirmed 46,XY karyotype, comprehensive investigations are expected to reveal a definitive endocrine or genetic abnormality in at least what proportion of cases?
items.explanation: Even with confirmed 46,XY, full biochemical and genetic workup finds a specific endocrine or genetic diagnosis in at least 25% — karyotype alone does not exclude DSD.
items.reference: In boys with 46,XY, comprehensive investigations will reveal an endocrine or genetic abnormality in at least a quarter of cases.

items.question: A male infant has isolated hypospadias with both gonads palpable in the scrotum. What is the approximate likelihood of an underlying chromosomal anomaly?
items.explanation: Isolated hypospadias carries approximately 7% chromosomal anomaly risk — higher than isolated cryptorchidism (3%) but lower than cryptorchidism plus hypospadias (13%).
items.reference: A chromosomal anomaly is present in approximately 7% of those with hypospadias.

items.question: According to DSD clinical evaluation criteria, an External Masculinization Score (EMS) below what threshold indicates that an infant should be considered for investigation for suspected DSD?
items.explanation: EMS <11 triggers consideration for suspected DSD investigation; 12 is typical male external genitalia.
items.reference: An EMS below 11 requires consideration for investigation for suspected DSD.

items.question: An External Genitalia Score (EGS) below which value requires further clinical evaluation for suspected DSD?
items.explanation: EGS <10.5 is the investigation threshold — parallel to EMS <11 on the same 0–12 scale.
items.reference: An EGS below 10.5 requires further clinical evaluation for suspected DSD.

items.question: Which specific type of isolated hypospadias is explicitly listed as requiring further investigation for suspected DSD?
items.explanation: Isolated perineal hypospadias warrants investigation; isolated glandular, coronal, and mid-shaft forms alone generally do not.
items.reference: Investigation is warranted for isolated perineal hypospadias.

items.question: Investigation for suspected DSD is warranted for which of the following familial presentations?
items.explanation: Any familial hypospadias lowers the threshold and warrants investigation — not limited to perineal or glandular forms.
items.reference: Any form of familial hypospadias warrants investigation for suspected DSD.

items.question: In newborn girls, a clitoral length greater than what measurement requires further clinical evaluation?
items.explanation: Clitoral length >8 mm triggers evaluation; clitoral length does not seem dependent on gestational age.
items.reference: A clitoral length greater than 8mm requires further clinical evaluation.

items.question: What is the statistical definition of micropenis provided in DSD evaluation criteria?
options: Stretched penile length less than 2.0 SD from the mean
Stretched penile length less than 2.5 SD from the mean
Stretched penile length less than 3.0 SD from the mean
Stretched penile length less than 1.5 SD from the mean
items.explanation: Micropenis = stretched penile length <2.5 SD; contemporary absolute newborn cut-off often <2 cm.
items.reference: Micropenis is a stretched penile length of less than 2.5 SD from the mean.

items.question: Based on contemporary studies cited in DSD evaluation criteria, what absolute measurement is a reasonable cut-off for micropenis in a newborn?
items.explanation: <2 cm is the practical newborn cut-off — e.g. 1.8 cm stretched length is clinically actionable.
items.reference: Less than 2cm is a reasonable cut-off in a newborn.

items.question: In approximately what percentage of affected cases is XY DSD part of a complex multi-system condition?
items.explanation: ~25% of XY DSD occurs within complex multi-system disorders — dysmorphic features lower investigation threshold.
items.reference: XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition in approximately 25% of affected cases.

items.question: Which isolated testicular presentation in boys warrants investigation for suspected DSD?
items.explanation: Bilateral undescended testes warrants investigation; unilateral inguinal testis alone generally does not.
items.reference: Bilateral undescended testes warrants investigation for suspected DSD.

items.question: Which isolated finding in a female newborn is explicitly stated to require further investigation?
items.explanation: Isolated clitoromegaly requires investigation; clitoral length >8 mm is the quantitative trigger (7 mm alone does not meet threshold).
items.reference: Isolated clitoromegaly in a female newborn requires further investigation.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT explicitly mentioned as a family history factor that lowers the threshold for DSD investigation?
items.explanation: Stillbirths, miscarriages, consanguinity, unexplained deaths, and need for steroid replacement lower threshold — early-onset diabetes is not listed.
items.reference: Family history factors include stillbirths, multiple miscarriages, and need for steroid replacement.

items.question: The presence of which of the following would lower the clinical threshold for investigating suspected DSD?
items.explanation: Systemic metabolic disorder associated with ambiguous genitalia lowers threshold; isolated glandular hypospadias and unilateral inguinal testis do not.
items.reference: A systemic metabolic disorder lowers the clinical threshold for investigation.

items.question: Why does the gestational age of a newborn girl not heavily alter the diagnostic threshold for clitoromegaly?
options: Because premature infants lack androgen receptors
Because clitoral length does not seem to be dependent on gestation
Because gestational age only impacts the External Genitalia Score (EGS)
Because DSDs do not manifest until term gestation is reached
items.explanation: The >8 mm clitoral threshold applies without gestational-age adjustment — clitoral length is not gestation-dependent per criteria.
items.reference: The length of the clitoris does not seem to be dependent on gestation.

items.question: How should a clinician manage a newborn boy with isolated glandular hypospadias and no other abnormal features or concerning family history?
options: Initiate a comprehensive genetic and endocrine DSD workup
Measure stretched penile length to rule in micropenis regardless of appearance
Lower the threshold for metabolic screening
items.explanation: Isolated glandular hypospadias without red flags does not meet suspected DSD investigation criteria — avoid unnecessary workup.
items.reference: Avoid unnecessary detailed investigations in boys with isolated benign findings such as glandular hypospadias.

items.question: Why does the guideline specify clinical cut-offs such as EMS <11, EGS <10.5, and isolated perineal hypospadias?
options: To mandate surgical intervention in all identified infants
To define the exact parameters of complex multi-system conditions
To identify infants requiring evaluation while avoiding unnecessary investigation of boys with isolated benign findings
To establish the 2.5 SD threshold for normal genital development across all sexes
items.explanation: Explicit triggers balance sensitivity for true DSD against over-investigation of low-risk isolated variants (e.g. unilateral inguinal testis).
items.reference: To isolate patients requiring evaluation, thereby avoiding unnecessary detailed investigations of boys with isolated benign findings.

items.question: How does documented family history of consanguinity or unexplained deaths influence the approach to an infant with mild atypical genitalia?
options: It automatically confirms a diagnosis of XY DSD
It lowers the clinical threshold required to initiate further investigation
It invalidates the use of the External Masculinization Score (EMS)
It requires immediate classification as a systemic metabolic disorder
items.explanation: Consanguinity, stillbirths, miscarriages, and unexplained deaths lower threshold — they do not confirm diagnosis alone.
items.reference: Family history of consanguinity or unexplained deaths lowers the clinical threshold for investigation.

items.question: Why is a stretched penile length of 1.8 cm in a newborn considered clinically actionable per DSD evaluation criteria?
options: Because it falls below the 2.5 SD / 2 cm reasonable cut-off for micropenis, which requires investigation
Because it indicates a systemic metabolic disorder
Because it automatically results in an EMS of less than 11
Because it is indicative of a complex multi-system condition in 25% of cases
items.explanation: 1.8 cm <2 cm newborn cut-off → micropenis → investigate; does not require EMS calculation to act.
items.reference: Less than 2cm is a reasonable cut-off; micropenis requires investigation.

items.question: How should a physician proceed if a newborn boy has isolated mid-shaft hypospadias without other abnormal features?
options: Classify as suspected DSD and begin investigations
Screen the family for a history of genital surgery
Calculate phallus length SD to ensure it is greater than 2.5 SD
items.explanation: Isolated mid-shaft hypospadias alone does not meet criteria for suspected DSD evaluation — perineal hypospadias differs.
items.reference: Avoid unnecessary detailed investigations for isolated mid-shaft hypospadias without other features.

items.question: Why might dysmorphic features in an infant with ambiguous genitalia change the management plan?
options: Because dysmorphic features prove familial hypospadias
Because they lower the threshold for investigation, likely reflecting that ~25% of XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition
Because dysmorphic features indicate an EGS above 10.5
Because they redefine the statistical parameters of micropenis
items.explanation: Dysmorphism suggests syndromic/multi-system disease — lower threshold to investigate even with mild genital findings.
items.reference: XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition in approximately 25% of affected cases.

items.question: An infant has an External Genitalia Score (EGS) of 10.0. How should the clinical team respond based on DSD evaluation criteria?
options: Discharge — score is within normal limits
Consider investigation for suspected DSD because the score is less than 10.5
Investigate only if there is a family history of hernias
Bypass investigation and proceed to steroid replacement
items.explanation: EGS 10.0 <10.5 threshold → consider suspected DSD workup regardless of other features until evaluated.
items.reference: An EGS below 10.5 requires further clinical evaluation for suspected DSD.

items.question: A cohort of 60 infants has definitive XY DSD. Based on epidemiological data (~25% part of complex multi-system conditions), how many are statistically expected to have multi-system involvement?
items.explanation: 25% of 60 = 15 infants expected to have XY DSD as a component of a broader complex multi-system condition.
items.reference: XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition in approximately 25% of affected cases.

items.question: A newborn boy has mildly atypical genitalia that might otherwise be observed. Family history reveals first-cousin parents and an uncle who required genital surgery in childhood. How should management change?
options: Raise the diagnostic threshold — investigate only if severe metabolic acidosis develops
No change — only the infant's physical examination dictates investigation
Lower the threshold for investigation and pursue detailed DSD workup despite mild genital findings
Confirm isolated intra-uterine growth retardation without DSD testing
items.explanation: Consanguinity and family history of genital surgery lower the threshold — work up mild findings aggressively.
items.reference: Consanguinity and a family history of genital surgery lower the threshold for investigation.

items.question: Why should a thorough physical examination for dysmorphic features be performed in an infant with XY DSD?
options: Dysmorphic features are the primary cause of isolated micropenis
Dysmorphic features suggest coexisting multi-system disease and lower the threshold for investigating suspected DSD
Dysmorphic features are universally present in low-birthweight infants
Absence of dysmorphic features guarantees isolated DSD requiring no endocrine workup
items.explanation: ~25% of XY DSD is syndromic/multi-system — dysmorphism lowers investigation threshold even with mild genital anomalies.
items.reference: Dysmorphic features lower the threshold for investigation; 25% of XY DSD is part of a complex multi-system condition.

items.question: A newborn with XY DSD is small for gestational age; the maternal chart documents severe intra-uterine growth retardation (IUGR). How does the guideline characterize this relationship?
options: Rare coincidence — fetal growth does not influence sexual differentiation
Inversely related — XY DSD associates with fetal macrosomia
Well-reported association — birthweight knowledge is highly helpful in XY DSD evaluation
Diagnostic criterion that alone confirms systemic metabolic disorder
items.explanation: Low birthweight and IUGR are well-reported associations with XY DSD — record and interpret birthweight in the workup.
items.reference: Knowledge of birth-weight in XY DSD is very helpful given the well-reported association between low birthweight, intra-uterine growth retardation and XY DSD.

items.question: Which combination of familial traits provides the strongest evidence-based rationale for lowering the investigation threshold in suspected XY DSD?
options: Sibling with asthma, parent with hypertension, grandparent with type 2 diabetes
Parents of different ethnicities, twin births, grandfather with prostate cancer
Multiple miscarriages, unexplained infant deaths, and a cousin requiring steroid replacement
Family history of macrosomia, advanced maternal age, sibling with fractured femur
items.explanation: Miscarriages, unexplained deaths, and need for steroid replacement are explicit familial red flags in DSD evaluation criteria.
items.reference: Family history of multiple miscarriages, unexplained deaths, and need for steroid replacement lowers the threshold for investigation.

items.question: A newborn with atypical genitalia also has a coexisting systemic metabolic disorder. Practically, "lower the threshold for investigation" means the physician should:
options: Require more severe genital abnormalities before initiating comprehensive DSD workup
Be more willing to initiate comprehensive DSD workup even if genital anomalies are mild or borderline
Proceed directly to genital surgery without laboratory results
items.explanation: Lowering threshold = act on milder genital findings when metabolic or familial modifiers increase pre-test probability of DSD.
items.reference: A systemic metabolic disorder lowers the clinical threshold for investigation.

items.question: The mother reports a brother with fertility problems and another with delayed puberty. Why are these details relevant to an infant with ambiguous genitalia?
options: They prove isolated XY DSD without multi-system involvement
They are familial red flags that lower the clinical threshold for DSD investigation
They indicate the infant's birthweight will fall below the 5th percentile
They require immediate steroid replacement therapy for the newborn
items.explanation: Fertility problems and delayed puberty in relatives are listed family-history modifiers that lower investigation threshold.
items.reference: Family history of fertility problems and delayed puberty lowers the threshold for investigation.

items.question: An NICU team is deciding whether to initiate costly DSD workup for an infant with atypical genitalia. Which historical fact alone would justify lowering the investigation threshold per guideline?
items.explanation: Family history of stillbirths is an explicit modifier lowering DSD investigation threshold — not isolated Cesarean or gestational diabetes.
items.reference: A family history of stillbirths lowers the threshold for investigation.

items.question: Why is a family history of a relative requiring steroid replacement a critical trigger for early DSD investigation?
options: Because steroid replacement is standard treatment for IUGR
Because it is grouped with severe familial red flags suggesting genetic or metabolic etiology, lowering the investigation threshold
Because it implies the newborn will definitely require immediate hernia surgery
Because it occurs in exactly 25% of XY DSD cases
items.explanation: Steroid replacement need in a relative sits alongside consanguinity, unexplained deaths, and genital abnormalities as threshold-lowering family history.
items.reference: Need for steroid replacement in the family lowers the threshold for investigation.

items.question: A researcher designs a neonatal DSD screening protocol. Why should birthweight data collection be strongly emphasized?
options: High birthweight completely rules out complex multi-system conditions
Birthweight dictates the precise surgical technique for genital correction
There is a well-reported association between low birthweight/IUGR and XY DSD
Birthweight is the only metric identifying systemic metabolic disorder
items.explanation: Low birthweight and IUGR associate with XY DSD — birthweight is a helpful evaluation data point, not a sole diagnostic criterion.
items.reference: Knowledge of birth-weight in XY DSD is very helpful given the well-reported association between low birthweight, intra-uterine growth retardation and XY DSD.

items.question: A 12-hour-old term neonate has atypical genitalia. The resident plans an immediate serum 17-OHP to definitively rule out CAH. Why is this plan flawed?
options: 17-OHP is only useful for rarer CAH forms, not 21-hydroxylase deficiency
Serum 17-OHP is usually unreliable before 36 hours of age
Pelvic ultrasound must precede any blood draw
Plasma glucose must be corrected before 17-OHP can be measured
items.explanation: Serum 17-OHP before 36 h can mislead — combine with serial electrolytes, glucose, and repeat timing per protocol.
items.reference: Serum 17OHP is usually unreliable before the age of 36 hours.

items.question: A 48-hour-old neonate with bilateral impalpable gonads has normal electrolytes. The team concludes salt-losing CAH is excluded. What is the fundamental error?
options: Electrolytes are never abnormal in 21-hydroxylase deficiency
In salt-losing CAH, electrolytes usually do not become abnormal before day 4 of life
Normal electrolytes at 48 hours confirm salt-losing CAH
Electrolytes should only be measured if glucose is profoundly elevated
items.explanation: False reassurance at 48 h — serial electrolytes through days 4–7 are required in atypical genitalia workup.
items.reference: In the salt-losing form of CAH, serum electrolytes usually do not become abnormal before day 4 of life.

items.question: A 30-week gestation neonate at 48 hours has elevated serum 17-OHP and atypical genitalia. Best integration of this finding?
options: Diagnose 21-hydroxylase deficiency immediately and start lifelong steroids
Interpret with caution — 17-OHP may be falsely elevated in premature or sick neonates
Disregard the result and rely solely on baseline cortisol
Assume the elevation is exclusively due to a rarer CAH form
items.explanation: Prematurity and illness elevate 17-OHP — clinical correlation, serial electrolytes, and confirmatory testing required.
items.reference: 17OHP concentrations may be falsely elevated in premature or sick neonates.

items.question: A 5-day-old infant with atypical genitalia is in extremis with high CAH suspicion. Immediate steroids are ordered. What critical step must occur before the first dose?
options: Pelvic ultrasound to delineate internal genitalia
Collect and store further serum and urine samples of sufficient volume
Wait for PCR or FISH results
items.explanation: Store diagnostic samples before therapy — post-steroid samples cannot support accurate diagnosis.
items.reference: Further serum and urine samples should be collected and stored before starting therapy.

items.question: Only a very small blood volume can be obtained before emergency steroids in a critically ill infant. Which assay receives first priority?
items.explanation: Priority order: 17-OHP, testosterone, androstenedione, renin activity, aldosterone.
items.reference: Order of priority: 17OHP, testosterone, androstenedione, renin activity, and aldosterone.

items.question: When initiating first-tier investigation for bilateral impalpable gonads, what is the primary objective of pelvic ultrasound?
options: Exclude all rarer forms of CAH
items.explanation: First tier defines sex chromosomes and delineates internal genitalia by pelvic ultrasound.
items.reference: Delineate, by pelvic ultrasound, the internal genitalia.

items.question: Why does first-tier atypical genitalia protocol prioritize rapid exclusion of 21-hydroxylase deficiency?
options: It is the commonest cause of a life-threatening condition associated with atypical genitalia in the newborn
It is the only condition causing bilateral impalpable gonads
It is the sole cause of falsely elevated 17-OHP in premature infants
It is the only condition requiring PCR or FISH for diagnosis
items.explanation: Morbidity/mortality urgency — 21-OHD CAH is the commonest life-threatening association with atypical genitalia.
items.reference: 21-hydroxylase deficiency is the commonest cause of a life-threatening condition associated with atypical genitalia in the newborn.

items.question: A specialist centre sends PCR/FISH and serum 17-OHP on Monday morning for atypical genitalia. By when should results be available per specialist standards?
items.explanation: PCR/FISH and serum 17-OHP should be available within a maximum of two working days in specialist centres.
items.reference: Results should be available within a maximum of two working days in all specialist centres.

items.question: A resident proposes stimulated serum cortisol to rapidly rule in CAH in a premature newborn with atypical genitalia. Why is this sub-optimal?
options: Stimulated cortisol perfectly mimics 17-OHP, making it redundant
Cortisol is only useful if drawn before 36 hours
Baseline or stimulated cortisol can be difficult to interpret in newborns, especially premature infants
Cortisol assays always require larger volumes than renin assays
items.explanation: Cortisol interpretation is unreliable in premature neonates — prioritize 17-OHP, electrolytes, glucose, and karyotype per first-tier protocol.
items.reference: Baseline or stimulated serum cortisol concentrations can be difficult to interpret in the newborn especially in the premature infant.

items.question: Which combination must be included in first-tier SOP for atypical genitalia to screen for the commonest life-threatening complication?
options: Plasma glucose, serum 17-OHP, and serial measurement of electrolytes
Renin activity, androstenedione, and baseline cortisol
Serum 17-OHP and one-time potassium at 24 hours
items.explanation: First-tier biochemical screen for CAH in atypical genitalia: glucose, 17-OHP, serial electrolytes — plus karyotype and pelvic US.
items.reference: First tier should include plasma glucose, serum 17OHP and serial measurement of electrolytes.

items.statement: DSD includes congenital discordance between chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex, even without overt genital ambiguity.
items.explanation: The definition includes discordance without visible ambiguity.
items.reference: DSD includes discordance among chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex and can include cases without ambiguity.

items.statement: Mosaicism arises from post-zygotic mitotic errors, whereas chimerism reflects two genetic origins.
items.explanation: Mosaicism and chimerism differ in their origin.
items.reference: Mosaicism comes from one zygote with mitotic nondisjunction, while chimerism reflects two genetic origins.

items.statement: Sex determination establishes gonadal fate, while sex differentiation shapes internal and external genitalia.
items.explanation: These are distinct developmental steps.
items.reference: Sex determination sets gonadal fate, and differentiation shapes internal and external genitalia.

items.statement: Duplication of DAX1 can lead to gonadal dysgenesis.
items.explanation: DAX1 is dose sensitive and duplication is pathogenic.
items.reference: Duplication of DAX1 results in gonadal dysgenesis.

items.statement: The EMS score is used to grade undervirilization in 46,XY DSD, with a normal male score of 12.
items.explanation: EMS is designed for 46,XY undervirilization.
items.reference: EMS is used for 46,XY DSD and a normal male score is 12.

items.statement: Androgen exposure after 12 weeks of gestation tends to cause isolated clitoromegaly rather than complete labial fusion.
items.explanation: Timing determines the degree of virilization.
items.reference: Exposure after 12 weeks generally causes isolated clitoromegaly.

items.statement: A blind vaginal pouch in 46,XY DSD typically reflects impaired androgen action with intact AMH.
items.explanation: AMH regression with deficient androgen action produces the blind pouch.
items.reference: Blind vaginal pouch occurs when AMH is preserved but testosterone action is impaired.

items.statement: Micropenis in a term newborn is defined by stretched penile length below about 2.5 cm.
items.explanation: This cutoff approximates -2.5 SD for term newborns.
items.reference: Micropenis is defined as stretched penile length below -2.5 SD, about 2.5 cm at term.

items.statement: Palpable gonads in a newborn with ambiguous genitalia suggest the presence of testicular tissue.
items.explanation: Palpable gonads usually represent testes or ovotestes.
items.reference: Palpable gonads suggest testes or ovotestes in newborns with ambiguous genitalia.

items.statement: P450 oxidoreductase deficiency is a CAH variant that can cause genital ambiguity in both 46,XX and 46,XY individuals.
items.explanation: POR deficiency is the CAH variant affecting both sexes.
items.reference: P450 oxidoreductase deficiency can produce ambiguity in both 46,XX and 46,XY.

items.statement: Psychosexual development includes gender identity, gender role, and gender orientation.
items.explanation: These three elements define behavioral sex.
items.reference: Psychosexual development comprises gender identity, gender role, and gender orientation.

items.statement: Gonadectomy is recommended early for high-risk gonads such as those in mixed gonadal dysgenesis.
items.explanation: Tumor risk drives early removal in high-risk conditions.
items.reference: Gonadectomy is recommended at diagnosis for high-risk conditions such as mixed gonadal dysgenesis.

items.statement: The External Genitalia Score (EGS) uses the same 0-to-12 point structure as the EMS but with gender-neutral embryological terminology.
items.explanation: EGS (van der Zwan 2015) maps mathematically to EMS; descriptors replace masculinization/micropenis/hypospadias language.
items.reference: The EGS maps exactly to the mathematical structure of the EMS (ranging from 0 to 12) but utilizes strictly descriptive, gender-neutral embryological anatomy.

items.statement: In boys with confirmed 46,XY karyotype and atypical genitalia, comprehensive endocrine and genetic investigations reveal an abnormality in at least 25% of cases.
items.explanation: A quarter or more receive a definitive endocrine or genetic diagnosis despite normal XY karyotype — full workup remains mandatory.
items.reference: Comprehensive investigations will reveal an endocrine or genetic abnormality in at least a quarter of cases.

items.statement: An External Masculinization Score below 11 warrants consideration for investigation for suspected DSD.
items.explanation: EMS investigation threshold is <11; 12 is typical male external genitalia.
items.reference: An EMS below 11 requires consideration for investigation for suspected DSD.

items.statement: Isolated glandular hypospadias in a newborn boy without other features always requires comprehensive DSD genetic and endocrine investigation.
items.explanation: Isolated glandular (and mid-shaft) hypospadias alone — avoid unnecessary detailed investigation unless other triggers present.
items.reference: Avoid unnecessary detailed investigations of boys with isolated benign findings like glandular hypospadias.

items.assertion: Disorders of sex development include cases without overt genital ambiguity.
items.reason: DSD is defined by discordance among chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: DSD is defined by discordance among chromosomal, gonadal, or phenotypic sex and can include cases without ambiguity.

items.assertion: Mosaicism involves multiple cell lines derived from a single zygote.
items.reason: Mitotic nondisjunction after fertilization creates additional cell lines.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Mosaicism arises from post-zygotic mitotic nondisjunction creating multiple cell lines.

items.assertion: External genital virilization requires conversion of testosterone to DHT.
items.reason: DHT has higher affinity for the androgen receptor than testosterone.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: External genital virilization depends on DHT because it has stronger androgen receptor affinity.

items.assertion: Prader staging is primarily used for virilization in 46,XX infants.
items.reason: It grades the extent of external genital virilization in 46,XX DSD.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Prader staging grades virilization of external genitalia in 46,XX individuals.

items.assertion: A blind vaginal pouch can occur in 46,XY DSD.
items.reason: AMH regression can be intact while testosterone action is impaired.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the mechanism.
items.reference: Blind vaginal pouch occurs when AMH is intact but androgen action is defective.

items.assertion: 5alpha-reductase type 2 deficiency often presents with undervirilized genitalia.
items.reason: Conversion of testosterone to DHT is impaired.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the phenotype.
items.reference: 5alpha-reductase deficiency causes undervirilization due to impaired DHT formation.

items.assertion: A T/DHT ratio above 10 after hCG supports 5alpha-reductase deficiency.
items.reason: DHT fails to rise appropriately when conversion is defective.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the ratio.
items.reference: T/DHT ratio greater than 10 after hCG indicates impaired testosterone to DHT conversion.

items.assertion: P450 oxidoreductase deficiency can cause genital ambiguity in both sexes.
items.reason: This defect disrupts multiple steroidogenic enzymes.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the breadth of effects.
items.reference: P450 oxidoreductase deficiency affects steroidogenic enzymes and can cause ambiguity in both sexes.

items.assertion: Gender dysphoria reflects distress due to mismatch between identity and assigned sex.
items.reason: The distress may occur with or without a DSD diagnosis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion.
items.reference: Gender dysphoria is distress from mismatch between identity and assigned sex, and it can occur with or without DSD.

items.assertion: Hypospadias repair is usually recommended in infancy.
items.reason: Early surgery benefits psychosocial development and tissue pliability.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the recommendation.
items.reference: Hypospadias repair is ideally performed at 6 to 12 months to support psychosocial outcomes and tissue pliability.

items.assertion: Gonadectomy is recommended at diagnosis for 46,XY gonadal dysgenesis.
items.reason: These gonads carry a high risk for germ cell malignancy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the recommendation.
items.reference: High malignancy risk in 46,XY gonadal dysgenesis supports gonadectomy at diagnosis.

items.assertion: XX male syndrome presents with small testes and infertility.
items.reason: SRY translocation drives male phenotype but germ cell failure leads to high FSH.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the findings.
items.reference: XX male syndrome results from SRY translocation with small testes, infertility, and elevated FSH.

items.assertion: The External Genitalia Score (EGS) is preferred over EMS for initial documentation of atypical genitalia before sex of rearing is assigned.
items.reason: EMS terminology such as masculinization, micropenis, and hypospadias assumes a male anatomical baseline, whereas EGS uses gender-neutral embryological descriptors with identical scoring weights.
items.explanation: Both true — EGS addresses the gender bias limitation of EMS while preserving the 0–12 quantitative framework.
items.reference: Using terms like masculinization, micropenis, or hypospadias assumes a male baseline, which is problematic before a definitive diagnosis or sex of rearing is assigned.
